refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall provide an integrated performance requirement derivation, recording, and management system that translates worksite mission context (site conditions, operational policies, safety constraints) into documented performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability), maintains a complete audit trail of baseline establishment and calibration updates, and ensures traceability from worksite inputs through performance targets to system design parameters.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit mention of traceability, audit trail, and the bidirectional linkage between worksite context and performance targets. Refinement clarifies that this is a closed-loop system requirement with documentation and historical management as first-class concerns, not afterthoughts. High safety criticality demands explicit traceability and change control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (1) worksite mission context is captured in a structured format; (2) performance targets are derived via documented method traceable to mission inputs; (3) baseline and calibration updates are recorded with timestamp, actor, and rationale; (4) traceability matrix links worksite inputs → performance targets → design parameters; (5) system prevents orphaned or undocumented performance targets.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Capture and structure worksite mission context (site conditions, operational policies, safety constraints) as input to performance derivation.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Derive performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from worksite mission context using a documented, repeatable method.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record and maintain a complete audit trail of performance target baselines and calibration updates with metadata (timestamp, actor, rationale).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Establish and maintain traceability from worksite inputs through performance targets to system design parameters.
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide query and reporting capability to retrieve performance targets and their derivation history for compliance and safety review.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized performance management database with structured schema for mission context, derived targets, and audit trail; coupled with a derivation engine that applies documented rules to transform mission inputs into performance targets; integrated with traceability linking module.

DP-B: Distributed performance target management where each Vehicle maintains its own performance targets derived locally from mission context pushed by Teleoperation; central registry aggregates and reconciles targets.
DP-C: Manual performance target derivation and recording via Site Manager interface with post-hoc audit trail capture; minimal automation, high human oversight.
DP-D: Hybrid approach: centralized derivation engine (DP-A core) with local caching in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules; synchronization protocol ensures consistency and audit trail integrity.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid centralized-distributed architecture with a core performance derivation and management engine (centralized, authoritative) coupled with local performance target caches in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules. The engine applies documented derivation rules to mission context inputs, generates performance targets, records all changes with full audit metadata, and maintains traceability links. Local caches enable offline operation and rapid access; a synchronization protocol ensures consistency and prevents orphaned targets. The engine is the single source of truth for audit trail and traceability.

potential_sources: Coupling between derivation engine and mission context capture (mitigated by well-defined input schema and validation rules).
Coupling between centralized engine and local caches (mitigated by explicit synchronization protocol and conflict resolution rules).
Coupling between performance targets and design parameters (mitigated by explicit traceability data structure and query interface).
coupling_check.notes: DP-D avoids tight coupling to any single physical or software module by defining the derivation engine as a logical service with clear input/output contracts. The hybrid approach balances centralized control (audit, traceability, consistency) with distributed resilience (local caching, offline capability). Decoupling is achieved through well-defined interfaces (mission context schema, synchronization protocol, traceability query API).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission context can be captured in a structured, machine-readable format. Risk: If mission context is highly unstructured or context-dependent, derivation rules may be brittle or incomplete.
Assumption: Derivation rules are stable and well-documented. Risk: If rules change frequently or are implicit in operator knowledge, audit trail and traceability become unreliable.

Assumption: Network connectivity is available for synchronization between centralized engine and local caches. Risk: If network is intermittent, synchronization delays may lead to stale targets or conflicts.

Assumption: Safety criticality can be managed through audit trail and traceability alone. Risk: If performance targets are incorrect at derivation time, audit trail does not prevent harm; independent validation of targets is needed.

Assumption: Local caches do not diverge significantly from centralized engine state. Risk: If synchronization fails or is delayed, local caches may become inconsistent, leading to conflicting performance targets across Vehicle and Teleoperation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Strengthen DP-D by explicitly adding: (1) a validation and approval gate for derived performance targets before they are committed to the audit trail (to address safety criticality); (2) a conflict detection and resolution protocol for synchronization failures; (3) a periodic reconciliation process to detect and flag divergence between centralized and local caches. These additions ensure that high safety criticality is addressed at the DP level, not deferred to implementation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the system-level container responsible for integrating all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and providing cross-cutting services including performance requirement derivation, recording, management, and traceability. This module owns the performance management engine, the audit trail database, the traceability matrix, and the synchronization protocol. It is the authoritative source for performance targets and their derivation history.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives mission context and operational directives from Site Manager and Tele-operator via Tasking interface. Mission context includes site conditions, operational policies, and safety constraints that drive performance target derivation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution outputs derived performance targets, baseline records, and calibration history via Telemetry interface to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules for local caching and operational use.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution exchanges safety-critical performance targets and validation status with Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface. Safety Services may flag performance targets as unsafe or require additional validation before commitment to audit trail.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution uses Network Link to synchronize performance targets and audit trail updates between centralized engine and local caches in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural system-level container for performance management. The derivation engine, audit trail, and traceability matrix are cross-cutting services that must be centralized for consistency and safety. Local caches in Vehicle and Teleoperation are acceptable diffusion (5%) because they are read-only replicas synchronized from the authoritative source. Ownership is clear: NGHE Solution owns the engine and audit trail; Vehicle and Teleoperation own their local caches.

Vehicle could host a local performance target cache (acceptable diffusion), but should NOT own the derivation engine or audit trail. If Vehicle owned the engine, each vehicle would derive targets independently, breaking traceability and audit trail integrity. High diffusion risk: performance targets would diverge across vehicles, and safety criticality could not be assured.

Teleoperation could host a local performance target cache for tele-operator access, but should NOT own the derivation engine or audit trail. If Teleoperation owned the engine, targets derived for remote operation might diverge from on-site targets, breaking consistency. High diffusion risk: tele-operator and on-site operator would see different performance targets, creating safety and coordination hazards.

Safety Services could own performance target validation and approval (a sub-function of CR-NGHE-005), but should NOT own the derivation engine or audit trail. If Safety Services owned the engine, performance targets would be derived with a safety-first bias, potentially missing operational efficiency or availability targets. Appropriate role: Safety Services validates derived targets and flags unsafe combinations before commitment to audit trail.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner because: (1) performance requirement derivation is a system-level concern that must integrate mission context from multiple stakeholders (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Safety Manager); (2) the audit trail and traceability matrix are cross-cutting services that must be centralized for consistency and compliance; (3) the derivation engine must be independent of any single operational mode (on-site vs. remote) to ensure targets are consistent across Vehicle and Teleoperation; (4) high safety criticality demands a single authoritative source for performance targets and their derivation history, not distributed ownership.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic system-level module with high complexity and tight coupling to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Mitigation: Define clear, minimal interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) and use synchronization protocol to decouple the engine from operational modules.

Risk: Performance derivation engine becomes a bottleneck if it is not scalable or responsive. Mitigation: Design the engine for high throughput and low latency; use local caches to reduce dependency on centralized engine for real-time operations.

Risk: Audit trail database grows unbounded and becomes difficult to query or maintain. Mitigation: Define retention policies, archival procedures, and indexing strategies for efficient query performance.

Risk: Synchronization protocol fails or is delayed, leading to stale or inconsistent performance targets in local caches. Mitigation: Implement conflict detection, resolution rules, and periodic reconciliation to detect and flag divergence.

Risk: Safety Services validation is bypassed or overridden, allowing unsafe performance targets to be committed to audit trail. Mitigation: Implement approval gate in the derivation engine that requires Safety Services sign-off before targets are finalized.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Strengthen the IM assignment by: (1) explicitly defining the synchronization protocol and conflict resolution rules as part of the NGHE Solution module specification; (2) adding a safety validation and approval gate as a sub-module within NGHE Solution that integrates with Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface; (3) defining retention and archival policies for the audit trail to manage database growth; (4) specifying performance targets (latency, throughput, availability) for the derivation engine to ensure it does not become a bottleneck.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid centralized-distributed architecture) directly addresses all aspects of the refined FR: (1) centralized derivation engine transforms mission context into performance targets; (2) audit trail captures all changes with metadata; (3) traceability links are maintained by the engine; (4) local caches enable distributed access while maintaining consistency through synchronization. The DP is logically sound and does not introduce unnecessary complexity. Coupling is managed through well-defined interfaces and protocols.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If the derivation engine is not properly isolated within NGHE Solution, it may become tightly coupled to Vehicle or Teleoperation modules, breaking the hybrid architecture. Mitigation: Define the engine as a service with explicit input/output contracts; use dependency injection or service locator pattern to decouple from operational modules.

Diffusion Risk 2: If local caches in Vehicle and Teleoperation are not synchronized regularly, they may diverge from the authoritative source, leading to inconsistent performance targets. Mitigation: Implement a synchronization protocol with explicit conflict detection and resolution rules; define synchronization frequency and timeout behavior.

Diffusion Risk 3: If the audit trail is not centralized in NGHE Solution, different modules may maintain separate audit trails, breaking traceability and compliance. Mitigation: Enforce that all performance target changes are recorded in the centralized audit trail; use event-driven architecture to propagate changes to local caches.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for the derivation engine and audit trail because it is the system-level module responsible for integrating all subsystems. Vehicle and Teleoperation are appropriate containers for local performance target caches because they need rapid access to targets for operational decisions. The containment hypothesis is sound: centralized engine and audit trail in NGHE Solution; distributed caches in Vehicle and Teleoperation; synchronization protocol ensures consistency.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-005 is feasible within NGHE Solution: (1) worksite mission context capture can be verified by inspecting the Tasking interface and input schema; (2) performance target derivation can be verified by tracing the derivation engine logic and comparing outputs to expected targets for known mission contexts; (3) audit trail can be verified by inspecting the audit trail database and confirming that all changes are recorded with metadata; (4) traceability can be verified by querying the traceability matrix and confirming that all performance targets are linked to mission inputs and design parameters; (5) query and reporting capability can be verified by executing sample queries and confirming that results are accurate and complete.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-005 to NGHE Solution is clear and unambiguous. The refined FR is decomposed into five sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5), each of which is allocated to a specific function within NGHE Solution: mission context capture (Tasking interface), derivation (engine), recording (audit trail), traceability (traceability matrix), and reporting (query interface). Ownership is clear: NGHE Solution owns all functions; Vehicle and Teleoperation own local caches as read-only replicas.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Add explicit safety validation and approval gate to DP-D. The gate should require Safety Services sign-off before performance targets are committed to the audit trail. This addresses high safety criticality and ensures that unsafe target combinations are detected and prevented.

Revision 2: Define synchronization protocol and conflict resolution rules as part of NGHE Solution specification. The protocol should specify synchronization frequency, timeout behavior, conflict detection criteria, and resolution rules. This mitigates diffusion risks and ensures consistency across Vehicle and Teleoperation caches.

Revision 3: Define performance targets (latency, throughput, availability) for the derivation engine. The engine must be responsive enough to support real-time operational decisions and not become a bottleneck. This ensures that the DP is implementable and does not introduce unacceptable delays.

Revision 4: Define retention and archival policies for the audit trail. The audit trail must be maintainable and queryable despite growing over time. This ensures that the DP is sustainable and does not degrade in performance as the system operates.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-005 is a complex, multi-faceted requirement that spans mission context capture, derivation logic, audit trail management, traceability, and reporting. The refined FR is decomposed into five atomic sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5), each of which requires further decomposition to define detailed design parameters and implementation modules. The selected DP (DP-D, hybrid centralized-distributed architecture) is logically sound and addresses the refined FR, but it is not atomic enough to proceed directly to implementation. Further decomposition is needed to define the derivation engine logic, audit trail schema, traceability data structure, synchronization protocol, and query interface. The gate review confirms that FR-DP and DP-IM mappings are sound, but recommends revisions to address safety criticality and diffusion risks. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify these revisions and enable detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Capture and structure worksite mission context (site conditions, operational policies, safety constraints) as input to performance derivation. [Requires decomposition into mission context schema, input validation, and capture interface.]

CR-NGHE-005.2: Derive performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from worksite mission context using a documented, repeatable method. [Requires decomposition into derivation rules, rule engine, and validation logic.]

CR-NGHE-005.3: Record and maintain a complete audit trail of performance target baselines and calibration updates with metadata (timestamp, actor, rationale). [Requires decomposition into audit trail schema, change capture mechanism, and metadata management.]

CR-NGHE-005.4: Establish and maintain traceability from worksite inputs through performance targets to system design parameters. [Requires decomposition into traceability data structure, link management, and query interface.]

CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide query and reporting capability to retrieve performance targets and their derivation history for compliance and safety review. [Requires decomposition into query language, reporting templates, and access control.]

revise.why: The selected DP (DP-D) is sound but requires explicit revision to address safety criticality and diffusion risks identified in the gate review. Specifically: (1) add a safety validation and approval gate that requires Safety Services sign-off before performance targets are committed to the audit trail; (2) define the synchronization protocol and conflict resolution rules explicitly; (3) define performance targets (latency, throughput, availability) for the derivation engine; (4) define retention and archival policies for the audit trail. These revisions strengthen the DP and reduce implementation risk.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Worksite mission context can be captured in a structured, machine-readable format with a well-defined schema.
Assumption 2: Performance target derivation rules are stable, well-documented, and can be implemented as a deterministic algorithm or rule engine.
Assumption 3: Network connectivity is available for synchronization between centralized engine and local caches in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.
Assumption 4: Safety Services can validate and approve performance targets in a timely manner without becoming a bottleneck.
Assumption 5: Local caches in Vehicle and Teleoperation can tolerate eventual consistency with the centralized engine (i.e., brief periods of stale targets are acceptable).
Assumption 6: The audit trail database can be scaled to handle the expected volume of performance target changes over the system lifetime.
open_risks: Risk 1 (High): If performance target derivation rules are incorrect or incomplete, the system may derive unsafe or infeasible targets. Mitigation: Implement independent validation of derived targets by Safety Services before commitment to audit trail.

Risk 2 (High): If synchronization between centralized engine and local caches fails or is delayed, Vehicle and Teleoperation may operate with stale or inconsistent performance targets. Mitigation: Implement conflict detection, resolution rules, and periodic reconciliation; define acceptable staleness thresholds.

Risk 3 (Medium): If the derivation engine becomes a bottleneck, real-time operational decisions may be delayed. Mitigation: Design the engine for high throughput and low latency; use local caches to reduce dependency on centralized engine.

Risk 4 (Medium): If the audit trail database grows unbounded, query performance may degrade and maintenance may become difficult. Mitigation: Define retention and archival policies; implement efficient indexing and query optimization.

Risk 5 (Medium): If the traceability matrix is not maintained consistently, it may become unreliable for compliance and safety review. Mitigation: Implement automated traceability link creation and validation; define procedures for updating links when requirements or design parameters change.

Risk 6 (Low): If the query and reporting interface is not user-friendly, Site Manager and Safety Manager may not be able to effectively retrieve and review performance targets and their derivation history. Mitigation: Conduct user research and usability testing; provide training and documentation.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Specification of worksite mission context schema, including all required fields, data types, and validation rules.
Evidence 2: Documentation of performance target derivation rules, including the algorithm or rule engine used to transform mission context into targets.
Evidence 3: Specification of the synchronization protocol, including synchronization frequency, timeout behavior, conflict detection criteria, and resolution rules.
Evidence 4: Specification of the audit trail schema, including all required metadata fields (timestamp, actor, rationale, change type, etc.).
Evidence 5: Specification of the traceability data structure, including how links are created, maintained, and queried.
Evidence 6: Performance targets (latency, throughput, availability) for the derivation engine, validated against operational requirements.
Evidence 7: Retention and archival policies for the audit trail, including retention periods, archival procedures, and deletion rules.
Evidence 8: Test cases and validation procedures for performance target derivation, including known mission contexts and expected targets.
Evidence 9: Safety validation procedures and approval criteria for performance targets, defined in collaboration with Safety Services.
Evidence 10: User interface mockups and usability test results for the query and reporting interface.
